title: Donation After Brain Death
desc: Brain death is declared. Half the family wants organ donation; the other half refuses, invoking faith and fear that 'they will kill him for organs while his heart still beats.' Media attention is building outside.

patient.name: Mehdi
patient.condition: Declared brain death after traumatic brain injury — donation-eligible

law.text: Organ procurement proceeds only within legal consent pathways; physicians must explain brain death clearly and resist both coercive donation pressure and obstructive misinformation.

complications.title: Reporter live outside
complications.liveLine: PA: "News crew wants a statement on whether you're 'expediting death for parts'."

complications.title: Two opposing advisers
complications.premise: One scholar supports donation; another forbids it.
complications.liveLine: Family shouts over speakerphone — Mehdi's brother collapses in a chair.

complications.title: OPO pushes clock
complications.liveLine: "We need a clear yes or the match list loses kidneys tonight — what is the family's final word?"

complications.title: Cousin with power of attorney
complications.premise: Old form may not reflect current law.
complications.liveLine: "I signed for him years ago — I say donate, period." Aunt: "You never spoke for him in life!"

steps.msg: Sister: "His heart is beating on the monitor — how is he dead? You're lying for organs." Nephew: "Dad wanted to donate — he told me."
steps.hint: Separate explaining death physiology from donation solicitation:

choices.label: Family meeting: brain death explained without donation talk
choices.reaction: Painful silence — but the myth 'heartbeat = alive' dissolves a little.

choices.label: Lead with transplant needs and waiting lists
choices.reaction: They hear sales pressure, not medical truth.

choices.label: Remove screens so family cannot see rhythms
Threaten security if family disagrees
choices.reaction: A viral video of your tone destroys public trust.

steps.msg: After explanation, dissent remains 3–3. No registered donor preference is found. OPO asks you to seek 'presumptive consent' angles — legally grey in your setting.
steps.q: How do you proceed legally and ethically?
steps.hint: Follow governing law; avoid stretching consent:

choices.label: Apply clear national consent rules; no donation if not met
choices.reaction: Tears — but no one is overridden by whisper campaigns.

choices.label: Stretch interpretation to authorize retrieval
choices.reaction: Legal complaint and family trauma haunt the program.

choices.label: Request additional mediation hours before final answer
choices.reaction: Some relatives shift — organ viability may be lost.

steps.msg: Family finally refuses donation unanimously after mediation. A young intern posts a frustrated tweet about 'wasted organs' without names.
steps.hint: Support staff education; protect families from shaming:

choices.label: Confidential debrief + social-media policy reminder
choices.reaction: The ward learns grief ethics without public scalding.

choices.label: Retweet to 'raise awareness'
choices.reaction: Family recognizes context — lawsuit and headlines explode.

debrief.summary: Organ donation ethics center on truthful brain-death education, lawful consent, and grieving-family support—not improvised coercion or social-media venting. Justice for wait-listed patients never authorizes bypassing clear family refusal rules.
points: Separate declaring death from requesting organs; mixed messages fuel conspiracy.
Religious diversity requires scholarship and time, not bulldozing minority relatives.
Utility pressure from OPO must not distort clinical honesty or legal consent.
Train trainees on grief, confidentiality, and professionalism online.
